question_text: At the equator, how does day length change throughout the year?

options.label: A
options.text: Day length changes significantly, with very long days in summer

options.label: B
options.text: Day length stays roughly 12 hours all year round

options.label: C
options.text: Days get progressively longer each month

options.label: D
options.text: Day length changes only during winter

correct_answer: Day length stays roughly 12 hours all year round
explanation: At the equator, the Sun is always roughly directly overhead at midday throughout the year because the equator is perpendicular to Earth's axis of tilt. This means the equator receives approximately equal amounts of sunlight every day, resulting in day length remaining constant at about 12 hours year-round. In contrast, locations at higher latitudes experience significant seasonal variations in day length due to Earth's axial tilt.
